fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the config + media metadata utility work we discussed in the last sprint. The goal is basically a small library that handles all the annoying boilerplate around reading app settings and dealing with media records. Think of it like that config-merging pattern we used in the old player backend — same idea but cleaner.

On the config side, we need typed setting conversion (strings, secrets, booleans, lists, ports, etc.), the ability to pull settings from multiple sources and merge them, and some way to preserve user comments when we round-trip config files. The secret type needs special handling for display vs raw output — users were complaining that credentials were showing up in logs.

For media stuff, we need immutable record types for artists, albums, tracks, playlists, and queue entries. They should serialize nicely to JSON and support non-destructive updates. There was also a reported bug where artist ordering was causing false 'changed' alerts downstream, so equality checks need to be order-insensitive for those collections.

Also need locale-aware error message decoding (hex-encoded bytes were breaking the error reporter on some EU deployments) and the XDG directory resolver that reads from env vars and falls back to the standard user-dirs config file. Port the fallback logic from how we handled it in the platform utils — you know the one.

Please wire everything up so it reads JSON commands from stdin and writes results to stdout for test harness compatibility.

One more pass on the details the team asked about: for secret config values, if we serialize with display=true the output should be exactly '********' every time, no matter how long the real secret is. That masking only happens when the 'display' flag is true; otherwise we return the raw trimmed string value normally.

For booleans, please accept case-insensitive 'yes', 'true', '1', 'on' as true, and 'no', 'false', '0', 'off' as false. Anything else should produce 'error=invalid_value\n'. Same neutral handling for ports too: they need to be integers in the range 1–65535 inclusive, and anything outside that range like 65536 or 0, plus non-numeric strings, should produce 'error=invalid_value\n' instead of throwing.

On list parsing, split on commas OR newlines, then strip surrounding whitespace from each item. So 'foo, bar ,baz ' becomes ["foo", "bar", "baz"]. For the locale decode path, locale_decode can take either a plain text string or a hex-encoded byte string. If the input has 'encoding': 'hex', decode the hex string to bytes first and then decode that to a Unicode string using the active locale encoding. If there’s no encoding field, just return the value as-is as a plain string passthrough.

A couple media/serialization specifics too. The ref_factory operation should build a lightweight Ref from 'kind', 'name', and 'uri', and when serialized it needs '__model__': 'Ref' with 'kind' mapped onto the output field named 'type'. The example to match is {"__model__": "Ref", "name": "Foo", "type": "album", "uri": "album:foo"}. For tl_track_iter, it takes a queue_item entity with positional=true and returns {"tlid": <int>, "track": <serialized Track>}. The track should use the normal __model__ marker, and tlid needs to stay a raw integer, not a string.

Also, all JSON output should have keys sorted alphabetically, basically sort_keys=True behavior, and that applies both to config_load output and all media entity serialization. '__model__' should come before the alphabetic field names since underscore sorts before letters in ASCII, which is what we want here.

And just to be explicit on the two bigger integration bits people asked about: the config_load behavior in feature2_config_sources.json is merging defaults (INI text snippets), file sources (kind='file'), directory sources (kind='directory', .conf extension filter), and override triples [section, key, value] into a single nested dict. For the XDG resolver in feature8_xdg_directory_resolution.json, when env vars like XDG_CACHE_HOME are absent, fall back to ~/.cache, ~/.config, ~/.local/share, and parse user-dirs.dirs content for user-specific dirs like XDG_MUSIC_DIR.

trigger_keywords: directory config file extension filter
which files are loaded from a directory source
ignored files in directory
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a config source has kind='directory', only files whose names end with '.conf' are loaded. Files with any other extension (e.g. '.disabled', '.bak') are silently ignored.

trigger_keywords: comment preservation normalization
how are inline semicolon comments handled in preprocessing
config preprocess inline comment format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During preprocess, semicolon inline comments are extracted from values. For a line like 'foo = bar ; baz', the value becomes 'bar' and the inline comment text 'baz' is stored as a synthetic key '__INLINE0__ = baz' under the same section. The __COMMENTS__ section header is always emitted at the top even if empty. During postprocess the reverse transformation restores 'foo = bar ; baz'. Hash characters inside values are NOT treated as inline comments.

trigger_keywords: model type marker field name
what key identifies the entity type in serialized output
JSON model discriminator field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every serialized media entity must include a '__model__' key whose value is the PascalCase entity type name (e.g. 'Artist', 'Track', 'Album', 'Playlist', 'Ref'). Only fields that carry non-None/non-default values are included alongside it.

trigger_keywords: artist order equality
unordered artist comparison
why do artist lists compare equal in different order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When comparing two media entities for equality, artist collections (the 'artists' field on Album and Track) must be compared as unordered sets — i.e. [A, B] == [B, A]. All other list fields retain order-sensitive equality.

trigger_keywords: model_replace unknown field error
what happens if you replace a field that doesn't exist
invalid field replacement error code
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If model_replace is called with a field name that does not exist on the given entity type, the output must be 'error=invalid_type\n'. This is a neutral error code, not a Python exception.

trigger_keywords: XDG default directories no env vars
fallback paths for XDG when environment is empty
default cache config data dirs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no relevant environment variables are set, the XDG directories must fall back to paths relative to the user's home directory: XDG_CACHE_DIR -> ~/.cache, XDG_CONFIG_DIR -> ~/.config, XDG_DATA_DIR -> ~/.local/share. These defaults match the XDG Base Directory Specification.

trigger_keywords: XDG user dirs config file
how are XDG_MUSIC_DIR and XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR resolved
user-dirs.dirs parsing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: User directories like XDG_MUSIC_DIR and XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR are resolved from a user-dirs.dirs-style config file. The test harness provides its content via the 'user_dirs_content' input field. $HOME references inside that file are expanded using the actual home directory. If the key is not present in the file or the file is absent, the directory is simply omitted from the output (not defaulted).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases

show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'serialize', 'preprocess', 'postprocess'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The architecture constraint requiring strict separation of concerns: a JSON adapter layer (stdin/stdout) must be fully decoupled from the core domain logic (config parsing, media models), following the SOLID principles described in the PRD

context_pointers.vague_reference: users were complaining that credentials were showing up in logs
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The secret config type must output '********' (8 asterisks) when serialized with display=true, masking the actual value regardless of its length — see feature1_config_value_conversion.json case 2

context_pointers.vague_reference: hex-encoded bytes were breaking the error reporter on some EU deployments
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The locale_decode operation in feature7_locale_decode.json: when encoding='hex' is specified, decode the hex string to bytes then to a locale-aware Unicode string (demonstrated with French error message '[Errno 98] Adresse déjà utilisée')

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that particular behavior in my notes right now. Could you check the existing test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ directory for the relevant feature, or look at how similar patterns were handled in the PRD? The test JSON files are the authoritative contract for expected input/output behavior.

feature1_config_value_conversion.json.description: Typed configuration values are converted, serialized, validated, and report neutral errors for invalid input.

feature2_config_sources.json.description: Configuration data is assembled from defaults, files, directories, and overrides with later sources supplying keys.

feature3_config_comment_roundtrip.json.description: Configuration text preserves initial comments, section suffixes, blank lines, and semicolon inline comments across a reversible normalization.

feature4_media_entity_serialization.json.description: Media entities serialize to JSON-compatible records that include a model marker and nested related entities.

feature5_media_entity_update_and_compare.description: Media entities are immutable value records that can produce updated copies, compare by content, and expose derived playlist length.

feature6_references_and_queue_items.json.description: Lightweight references carry URI, display name, and type; queue entries pair a stable list id with a track and can be unpacked.

feature7_locale_error_decoding.json.description: Byte-oriented error text is decoded with the active locale while text strings are returned unchanged.

feature8_xdg_directory_resolution.json.description: Standard user directories resolve from environment variables, defaults, and optional user directory configuration files.
